text: Abstract Bioactive peptides represent short sequences in proteins that are inactive within the intact protein and are activated upon release by enzymatic proteolysis within the gastrointestinal tract. In the recent years, the research on bioactive peptides from different sources such as plants, animals and microorganisms have attracted considerable interest. Bioactive peptides are generally prepared using hydrolysis by plant or microbial enzymes, digestive enzymes or fermentation using proteolytic starter culture. The wide range of functions exhibited by different peptides are attributed to their unique amino acid composition and sequences, including muscule relaxation, binding with biological molecules, immunostimulation, antioxidant, antimicrobial, antiinflammatory, hypocholesterolemic and antihypertensive activities. Generally, bioactive peptides are identified using membrane separation techniques and chromatography from protein hydrolysis products along with spectrometric techniques. In order for using bioactive peptides as health or therapeutic products, stability, bioavailability and safety of the peptides have to be ensured.
